Oat++.history: Oat++ is an open-source C++ web framework released in 2018. It provides high-performance HTTP server capabilities for building APIs and microservices in C++.
Oat++.summary_abuse: Oat++ has no abuse signal. It is a C++ web framework library with no consumer-facing surface.

Obsidian Incentivize.history: Obsidian Incentivize is a Shopify app for increasing average order value through incentive mechanics. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
Obsidian Incentivize.summary_abuse: Obsidian Incentivize is a Shopify incentive app with no documented abuse signal. Incentive mechanics can contribute to dark patterns but no specific incidents are documented.

Obsidian Publish.history: Obsidian Publish is a paid add-on service for the Obsidian note-taking app, developed by Dynalist Inc. co-founders Shida Li and Erica Xu. Obsidian launched in public beta in March 2020, with Publish available as a hosted note-sharing service. The company is independently funded and headquartered in Canada.
Obsidian Publish.summary_abuse: Obsidian Publish allows users to host notes publicly under the publish.obsidian.md domain. The free-to-publish nature with user-controlled subpaths could theoretically be used for phishing landing pages, though no documented misuse of this nature is reported and the service requires a paid subscription.

Obviyo.history: Obviyo was launched in 2021 as a rebrand of HiConversion, an ecommerce personalisation and A/B testing platform founded by serial entrepreneurs Zee Aganovic and David Henrickson in Boca Raton, Florida. The founders had backgrounds in startups acquired by Fortune 50 companies including Ricoh and Microsoft. The rebrand marked a pivot to a hyper-personalisation and optimisation focus for ecommerce merchants.
Obviyo.summary_abuse: Obviyo is an ecommerce personalisation and optimisation platform with no documented abuse history. It integrates with merchant websites to personalise shopping experiences but is a B2B subscription product not accessible for anonymous or fraudulent use.

Occasion.history: Occasion is an online booking and appointment scheduling SaaS platform. No notable founding year or corporate history is publicly documented.
Occasion.summary_abuse: Occasion has no notable abuse signal. It is an online booking platform for legitimate service businesses with no anonymous content hosting features.

Ocea.history: Ocea is a cloud-based PaaS for website and application creation. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
Ocea.summary_abuse: Ocea is a PaaS platform with no documented abuse signal.

OceanWP.history: OceanWP is a free multi-purpose WordPress theme created in 2016 as an open-source project. OceanWP LLC operates as a small independent company offering the free theme plus premium extensions. It has become one of the most popular WordPress themes with millions of active installs.
OceanWP.summary_abuse: OceanWP has no notable abuse signal as a WordPress theme. It is installed on legitimate websites as a design foundation. Like all popular free WordPress themes, it may be bundled in nulled theme packages, but the official theme itself is not implicated in fraud.

Ochanoko.history: Ochanoko (ocnk.com) is a Japanese ecommerce shopping cart and web hosting platform operated by Ochanoko-net Inc. based in Kobe, Japan. No notable founding year or ownership changes are publicly documented.
Ochanoko.summary_abuse: Ochanoko has no notable abuse signal outside Japan. It is a hosted ecommerce platform for Japanese merchants with no documented use in international fraud or phishing campaigns.

Oclocher.history: Oclocher is a French parish communication platform for Catholic parishes and dioceses. No notable founding year or corporate history is publicly documented.
Oclocher.summary_abuse: Oclocher has no notable abuse signal. It is a specialized communication platform for Catholic parish communities with no consumer-facing content hosting relevant to fraud.

ocStore.history: ocStore is a Ukrainian-community fork of OpenCart, an open-source ecommerce platform. It is primarily used in Russian and Ukrainian-speaking markets and closely tracks the upstream OpenCart codebase.
ocStore.summary_abuse: ocStore is an open-source ecommerce fork used mainly in Eastern European markets. Like any ecommerce platform it could be deployed by fraudsters for fake storefronts, but no systematic abuse pattern specific to ocStore is documented.

Oct8ne.history: Oct8ne is a visual customer service and live chat platform founded in Spain, offering co-browsing and product visualization tools for ecommerce. No notable corporate history or ownership changes are documented.
Oct8ne.summary_abuse: Oct8ne has no notable abuse signal. It is a visual customer service tool for ecommerce businesses with no consumer-facing content hosting or anonymous access features.

Octane.history: Octane was founded in 2014 as a fintech lending platform specializing in consumer financing for powersports, motorcycles, ATVs, and outdoor power equipment. It operates through its in-house lender Roadrunner Financial and has raised significant VC funding including a $500M facility.
Octane.summary_abuse: Octane is a consumer lending platform for powersports purchases. Financial lending platforms can be exploited for loan application fraud or identity theft. Octane has standard KYC and lending compliance requirements. No specific documented abuse incidents are known.

Octane AI.history: Octane AI was founded in 2016 and grew as a Shopify-focused quiz and product recommendation platform backed by General Catalyst, Bullpen Capital, and Boost VC. It was recognized as Shopify's Best Storefront App in 2021.
Octane AI.summary_abuse: Octane AI has no notable abuse signal. It is a quiz and personalization tool for ecommerce merchants with no anonymous content hosting features.

Octipulse.history: Octipulse is a content management and cross-channel publishing platform for businesses. No notable founding year or corporate history is publicly documented.
Octipulse.summary_abuse: Octipulse has no notable abuse signal. It is a content management SaaS for business communications with no consumer-facing hosting features.

October CMS.history: October CMS was created by Alexey Bobkov and Samuel Georges and first released in 2014 as a free, open-source CMS built on top of the Laravel PHP framework. It grew a modest but loyal community and added a marketplace for plugins. In 2021, the project introduced a paid license model for new versions while keeping older versions open-source, which caused some community controversy.
October CMS.summary_abuse: October CMS is a self-hosted open-source CMS with minimal documented abuse as a platform. Like any self-hostable CMS it could be installed to host malicious content on a user's own server, but October CMS itself is not a hosted service and has no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters target directly.

Octopress.history: Octopress was created by Brandon Mathis around 2009 as a blogging framework layered on top of Jekyll. It gained popularity among developers as a way to publish static blogs to GitHub Pages. The project became largely dormant after 2015, with Mathis acknowledging in 2015 that Octopress 3 was in development but effectively the project was abandoned. Jekyll matured significantly, reducing the need for Octopress as a separate layer.
Octopress.summary_abuse: Octopress is an abandoned static site generator with no notable abuse signal. It generates static HTML files deployed to hosting of the user's choice, with no centrally-hosted consumer surface.

Octorate.history: Octorate was founded in 2012 and is headquartered in Rome, Italy. The company provides an all-in-one hotel management suite including a PMS, RMS, channel manager, and booking engine, processing over 500,000 bookings per month across 100+ countries. Octorate is part of the Dylog Buffetti Group, one of Italy's leading technology companies, which provides the platform with enterprise-grade infrastructure stability.
Octorate.summary_abuse: Octorate is an Italian hotel channel manager and PMS platform serving hospitality businesses. It has no documented abuse history. The B2B subscription model requires verified hotel operator accounts, making it an unlikely target for phishing or fraud activity.

Ocuco FitMix.history: Ocuco is an Irish software company specializing in optical and eyecare practice management. FitMix is their virtual frame try-on product, built in partnership with FittingBox technology, added to Ocuco's suite for optical retailers.
Ocuco FitMix.summary_abuse: Ocuco FitMix has no notable abuse signal. It is a virtual try-on tool for optical retailers with no consumer-facing hosting or anonymous access features.

Ocular.history: Ocular is a video and live commerce platform for ecommerce brands. No notable founding year or corporate history is publicly documented.
Ocular.summary_abuse: Ocular has no notable abuse signal. It is a live commerce platform for legitimate ecommerce brands with no consumer-facing anonymous content hosting.

Oculizm.history: Oculizm is a conversion optimization tool using user-generated content and reviews. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
Oculizm.summary_abuse: Oculizm is a reviews and UGC conversion tool with no documented abuse signal.

Oddcast.history: Oddcast is a US-based platform offering avatar-based video and marketing products for SMBs, operating since the early 2000s. No notable ownership changes are documented.
Oddcast.summary_abuse: Oddcast has no notable abuse signal. It is a video and avatar marketing tool for SMBs with no consumer-facing content hosting that would attract fraudsters.

Oddle.history: Oddle is a Singapore-based online ordering and restaurant management SaaS platform. No notable founding year or ownership changes are publicly documented.
Oddle.summary_abuse: Oddle has no notable abuse signal. It is a restaurant ordering SaaS for F&B businesses with no consumer-facing anonymous content hosting.

Odeum.history: Odeum is a SaaS platform for branded video subscription services. No notable founding year or corporate history is publicly documented.
Odeum.summary_abuse: Odeum has no notable abuse signal. It is a video subscription platform for content creators and businesses with standard content moderation typical of video hosting platforms.

Odoo.history: Odoo was founded in 2005 in Belgium by Fabien Pinckaers as TinyERP, then renamed OpenERP in 2009, and finally rebranded as Odoo in 2014. It grew to become one of the most widely used open-source ERP platforms globally, with a dual-license model offering a free Community edition and a paid Enterprise edition. The company raised significant funding and has over 7 million users worldwide.
Odoo.summary_abuse: Odoo is a widely used open-source ERP platform with no notable abuse signal as a product. Its self-hosted nature means abuse risk is inherited by the sites deploying it, not by Odoo itself. No documented campaigns specifically exploit Odoo's infrastructure.

Offline.js.history: Offline.js was created by HubSpot and released around 2013 as an open-source JavaScript library that automatically detects and alerts users when they lose internet connectivity. The project has had limited activity in recent years but remains in use across many websites.
Offline.js.summary_abuse: Offline.js is a simple client-side JavaScript library with no abuse signal. It performs only browser-side connectivity detection and has no server-side surface fraudsters could target.

Offset.history: Offset is a wine ecommerce platform providing specialized solutions for wine merchants and direct-to-consumer wineries. No notable founding year or corporate history is publicly documented.
Offset.summary_abuse: Offset has no notable abuse signal. It is a specialized ecommerce platform for wine merchants with age-gating and compliance requirements that reduce misuse risk.

Oh Dear.history: Oh Dear was founded in Belgium by Freek Van der Herten and Mattias Geniar, launching around 2017. It is a developer-friendly website monitoring SaaS covering uptime, SSL, broken links, scheduled tasks, DNS, and domain expiry checks. It has remained bootstrapped and independently operated.
Oh Dear.summary_abuse: Oh Dear is a B2B website monitoring tool with no documented abuse signal. It monitors external URLs on behalf of paying customers and has no consumer-accessible content hosting or anonymous signup surface.

Ohava.history: Ohava is a business website and marketing management platform. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
Ohava.summary_abuse: Ohava is a business website management SaaS with no documented abuse signal.

Okendo.history: Okendo was founded in 2016 in Sydney, Australia as a customer reviews and loyalty platform for Shopify merchants. It achieved Built for Shopify status and serves over 18,000 brands including SKIMS and Rhode.
Okendo.summary_abuse: Okendo has no notable abuse signal. It is a customer reviews and loyalty platform deployed on legitimate ecommerce sites with no consumer-facing anonymous content hosting features.

OkMenu.history: OkMenu is a restaurant QR menu SaaS launched around 2020, targeting the hospitality industry's shift to digital menus. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
OkMenu.summary_abuse: OkMenu is a restaurant QR menu SaaS with no documented abuse signal. Its use case is limited to hospitality businesses.

Okta.history: Okta was founded in 2009 in San Francisco by Todd McKinnon and Frederic Kerrest as an enterprise identity and access management platform. It went public on NASDAQ in 2017 (OKTA). In 2021, Okta acquired Auth0 for $6.5 billion to expand its developer identity business. In October 2023, Okta disclosed a support system breach where attackers accessed customer support tickets, affecting a significant number of enterprise customers.
Okta.summary_abuse: Okta is a dominant identity platform used by thousands of enterprises. It is a target of sophisticated attackers seeking to compromise corporate SSO, and has experienced notable breaches including a 2023 support system compromise and the 2022 LAPSUS$ incident where an internal laptop was accessed. Legitimate use overwhelmingly dominates, and Okta maintains extensive security certifications, a bug bounty program, and a public trust portal. Abuse risk is on the receiving end (Okta as a target) rather than Okta being misused as a tool.

Olapic.history: Founded 2010 in New York by Jose De Cabo as a visual UGC marketing platform. Acquired by Monotype for approximately $130M in 2016, then sold to Social Native in September 2020.
Olapic.summary_abuse: Olapic is a UGC content curation and visual marketing platform. It has no notable abuse signal; it serves enterprise retail brands in aggregating user-generated content and has no consumer-facing features that fraudsters typically target.

Olark.history: Founded 2009 by Ben Congleton, Zach Steindler, Matt Pizzimenti, and Roland Osborne through Y Combinator. The company remained independent and self-funded to millions in ARR. No notable acquisitions or rebrands.
Olark.summary_abuse: Olark is a live chat platform for customer support on websites. It has no notable abuse signal; it is a B2B tool used by businesses to interact with website visitors and is not associated with phishing, spam, or fraudulent use cases.

Oleoshop.history: Development began 2011 in Barcelona, Spain, first public version launched April 2014. Oleoshop is a Spanish-language SaaS e-commerce platform for SMBs in Spain and Latin America. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes documented.
Oleoshop.summary_abuse: Oleoshop is a Spanish e-commerce platform targeting SMBs. It has no notable abuse signal; e-commerce platforms carry a general risk of hosting fake storefronts, but Oleoshop has no documented incidents and is primarily used legitimately by small businesses.

Olvy.history: Olvy was founded around 2021 as a user feedback management SaaS for product teams. In November 2024 the founder announced the platform would be shutting down, officially deprecating the service.
Olvy.summary_abuse: Olvy is a product feedback management tool with no notable abuse signal. It does not host arbitrary user content, provide mass-email or file-hosting capabilities, or expose features that fraudsters typically exploit. The service has been deprecated as of late 2024.

Omacro.history: Omacro is a SaaS dealer-locator and where-to-buy platform for brands and suppliers. The company has limited public history documentation; no notable acquisitions or ownership changes are known.
Omacro.summary_abuse: Omacro is a dealer-locator platform that connects buyers with authorized retailers. It has no notable abuse signal; it is a B2B marketing tool with no consumer-facing features commonly exploited for fraud.

Omeda.history: Founded 1978 in Chicago, Illinois. Omeda is an audience data and marketing automation platform serving media companies and publishers. The company received a growth investment from Sverica Capital Management.
Omeda.summary_abuse: Omeda is an enterprise audience data and marketing automation platform for media companies. It has no notable abuse signal; it serves B2B media industry clients and has no consumer-facing features typically exploited for fraud.

Omega Commerce.history: Founded 2015 in Hanoi, Vietnam as one of the first Shopify app development companies in Vietnam. Omega Commerce develops apps for Shopify merchants including search, shopping feeds, and reporting tools. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes.
Omega Commerce.summary_abuse: Omega Commerce is a suite of Shopify-ecosystem apps for product discovery and reporting. It has no notable abuse signal; it is a B2B tooling provider for Shopify merchants with no consumer-facing features commonly exploited for fraud.

Omeka.history: Omeka was created by the Roy Rosenzweig Center for History and New Media at George Mason University. Development was funded by the Institute of Museum and Library Services starting in 2007, with the first release in February 2008. Since 2016, the Omeka project has operated as an independent entity under the fiscal stewardship of Digital Scholar, a nonprofit dedicated to sustaining open-source digital humanities infrastructure. The platform has been downloaded over 500,000 times and is used by libraries, archives, and museums worldwide.
Omeka.summary_abuse: Omeka is an open-source digital collections and exhibit platform used by academic institutions, libraries, and museums. It has no notable abuse signal — it is primarily self-hosted and does not expose consumer-facing content hosting, mass-email, or anonymous signup features that attract fraudsters.

Ometria.history: Founded 2013 in London by Ivan Mazour, Djalal Lougouev, James Dunford Wood, and Alastair James. Ometria raised $6M in 2017, then $40M Series C in 2021 led by InfraVia. The company targets retail brands with AI-driven customer marketing.
Ometria.summary_abuse: Ometria is a B2B customer data and marketing automation platform for retail brands. It has no notable abuse signal; it is used by retailers to manage customer data and send targeted communications, with no consumer-facing features commonly exploited for fraud.

Ometrics.history: Ometrics was founded by Greg Ahern, a CRO consultant active in digital marketing since 1994. The company developed the Ochatbot AI chatbot product and offers exit popups, A/B testing, and surveys. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes.
Ometrics.summary_abuse: Ometrics is a CRO and AI chatbot platform for SMB websites. Exit popups and lead-capture sliders are mildly intrusive but standard in the CRO industry. No documented abuse incidents. Not commonly associated with fraud or phishing.

Omie.history: Founded 2013 by Marcelo Lombardo and Rafael Olmos in Sao Paulo, Brazil. Omie is a cloud-based ERP and CRM platform for Brazilian SMBs. The company raised $160M led by Partners Group and has grown to serve tens of thousands of small businesses in Brazil.
Omie.summary_abuse: Omie is a Brazilian ERP and CRM platform for SMBs. It has no notable abuse signal; it is a backend business management system with no consumer-facing features typically exploited for fraud or phishing.

Omise.history: Founded June 2013 by Jun Hasegawa and Ezra Don Harinsut in Thailand. Omise is a payment gateway serving Thailand, Japan, and Singapore. The company later rebranded as OpnPayments to reflect broader market expansion.
Omise.summary_abuse: Omise is a Southeast Asian payment gateway. Payment processors carry a general risk of being used to process fraudulent transactions, but Omise has no documented incidents of systematic abuse. PCI DSS compliance and standard fraud detection controls apply.

Omni CMS.history: Originally developed by Omni Update, OU Campus was a CMS purpose-built for higher education institutions. Modern Campus acquired Omni Update and rebranded the product as Omni CMS, then later as Modern Campus CMS, integrating it into a broader student lifecycle management platform.
Omni CMS.summary_abuse: Omni CMS is a higher-education CMS with no notable abuse signal. It is used by colleges and universities for institutional web management, with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters typically target.

Omniconvert.history: Founded in Romania around 2013 under the name Marketizator, the company rebranded to Omniconvert and received 1 million EUR investment from Catalyst Romania. It is known as one of the first CRO platforms to bundle A/B testing, surveys, and web personalization in one tool.
Omniconvert.summary_abuse: Omniconvert is a CRO and A/B testing platform with no notable abuse signal. It is used primarily by e-commerce businesses for website optimization and has no consumer-facing hosting surface that would attract fraudsters.

Omnikick.history: Omnikick is a marketing automation and customer engagement platform. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
Omnikick.summary_abuse: Omnikick is a marketing automation platform. Like all marketing automation tools, it can potentially be used for spam campaigns, but no specific documented abuse incidents are known.

Omnisend.history: Founded in 2014 as Soundest by Rytis Lauris and Justas Kriukas in Lithuania, the company rebranded to Omnisend in November 2017 to reflect its omnichannel marketing focus. It raised its first seed round of $1.6M in 2019 after five bootstrapped years and grew to $55M in revenue with 100,000 customers by 2024.
Omnisend.summary_abuse: Omnisend is a mainstream email and SMS marketing platform primarily serving e-commerce merchants. Like all bulk email senders, it has some risk of misuse for spam campaigns, but the platform enforces anti-spam policies and requires list consent. No major abuse incidents are publicly documented.

Omnisend Email Marketing & SMS.history: This is the Shopify App Store distribution channel for Omnisend, the email and SMS marketing automation platform founded in 2014 as Soundest and rebranded in 2017. The Shopify app specifically targets Shopify merchants with omnichannel marketing tools.
Omnisend Email Marketing & SMS.summary_abuse: As a Shopify-integrated email and SMS marketing tool, Omnisend Email Marketing & SMS carries the typical bulk-sender abuse risk of marketing automation platforms. The Shopify App Store approval process and Omnisend's own anti-spam policies limit but do not eliminate misuse. No major documented abuse incidents.

Omny Studio.history: Founded in 2012 in Melbourne by Andrew Armstrong, Ed Hooper, and Long Zheng, Omny Studio launched its enterprise podcast hosting platform in 2015 after earlier audio projects. Triton Digital formed a strategic partnership with Omny in 2017 and completed an acquisition in June 2019, integrating Omny into Triton's audio technology portfolio.
Omny Studio.summary_abuse: Omny Studio is an enterprise podcast hosting and audio management platform primarily used by radio stations and broadcasters. It has no notable abuse signal and no consumer-facing free hosting tier that would attract fraudulent content.

Omurga Sistemi.history: Omurga Sistemi is a Turkish CMS and web solutions platform operated by OS Bilisim. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
Omurga Sistemi.summary_abuse: Omurga Sistemi is a Turkish CMS platform with no documented abuse signal.

On The Map.history: On The Map, Inc. was founded in the fall of 2009 by Rick Hoskins in Miami, Florida, as a digital marketing agency offering SEO, online marketing, and website development services to businesses across the US. The company grew from a local SEO agency to a broader digital marketing platform offering local visibility and multi-location presence management.
On The Map.summary_abuse: On The Map is a US digital marketing agency and local visibility platform. There is no documented abuse history. As an agency managing business listings and SEO, it has minimal consumer-facing attack surface that would attract phishing or fraud operators.

OnChat.history: OnChat is an AI chatbot-based customer support SaaS. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
OnChat.summary_abuse: OnChat is an AI live chat SaaS with no documented abuse signal.

Ondestek.history: Ondestek is a Turkish live chat and customer support software. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
Ondestek.summary_abuse: Ondestek is a live chat SaaS with no documented abuse signal.

One.com.history: Founded in 2002 by Jacob Jensen in Denmark as B-One, the company rebranded to One.com and grew to serve over 1.5 million customers in 149 countries within 20 years. It expanded to offices in five European countries with over 300 employees.
One.com.summary_abuse: One.com is a shared web hosting provider used by millions of customers. Like all budget shared hosting providers, its shared infrastructure can be used to host phishing pages or malware if account-level abuse controls are insufficient. No major public incidents are known, but shared hosting at scale always carries some abuse risk.

OneAll.history: OneAll is a social login and identity aggregation API service. It provides social authentication connections for websites, allowing users to sign in via third-party identity providers. Limited public founding history is available.
OneAll.summary_abuse: OneAll is a social login API with no notable abuse signal. Social login intermediaries can in theory be misused for credential harvesting, but OneAll operates as a standard OAuth proxy with no documented abuse incidents.

OneAPM.history: Founded in 2008 in Beijing by He XiaoYang and Huang Dong under the name 蓝海讯通 (Lanhai Xuntong), OneAPM raised $37.9M across multiple funding rounds including a Series C. It targets Chinese enterprise customers with APM and IT operations monitoring solutions.
OneAPM.summary_abuse: OneAPM is an application performance monitoring platform with no notable abuse signal. It is a backend infrastructure monitoring tool with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.

OneCause.history: Founded in 2008 in Indianapolis as BidPal, pioneering mobile bidding for nonprofit galas, the company rebranded to OneCause to reflect its broader fundraising platform offering. By 2024 it had helped nonprofits raise over $8 billion across 90,000 events.
OneCause.summary_abuse: OneCause is a nonprofit fundraising platform with no notable abuse signal. It is used exclusively by registered nonprofits for charitable fundraising with payment processing, and is not a surface targeted by scammers.

OneCommerce.history: OneCommerce is an ecommerce conversion optimization SaaS. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
OneCommerce.summary_abuse: OneCommerce is an ecommerce SaaS with no documented abuse signal.

Onehub.history: Founded in 2007 in Seattle as a secure file sharing and virtual data room platform for businesses. Over 1 million customers have used it to share more than 4 billion files. No major acquisitions or ownership changes are publicly documented.
Onehub.summary_abuse: Onehub is a secure business file sharing and virtual data room service. Its business-oriented access controls and encryption-first design mean it has minimal consumer-facing exposure and no documented abuse signal.

Onepage.history: Onepage is a page-building SaaS for creating landing pages and one-page websites. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
Onepage.summary_abuse: Onepage is a page builder SaaS. Like all hosted page builders, it could theoretically be used to host phishing or scam landing pages, but no specific documented incidents are known.

OnePage Express.history: OnePage Express is a WordPress theme for creating one-page websites, offering freemium and paid tiers. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
OnePage Express.summary_abuse: OnePage Express is a WordPress theme with no documented abuse signal.

OnePress Social Locker.history: OnePress Social Locker is a WordPress plugin that hides content behind social sharing buttons, used to grow social media followings by requiring a tweet, like, or share to unlock content. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
OnePress Social Locker.summary_abuse: OnePress Social Locker is a social-share gate WordPress plugin. It can be used to force social interactions before revealing content, a pattern considered manipulative. No documented abuse incidents at the platform level, but the mechanism itself is a dark-pattern-adjacent design.

OneSignal.history: Founded in 2015 by George Deglin and Long Vo after pivoting from a Y Combinator-backed mobile game studio, OneSignal became the market leader in push notifications. It raised $25M Series B in 2019, $50M Series C in 2022, and has 1.3 million customers by 2024 with $84M total funding.
OneSignal.summary_abuse: OneSignal is a push notification and messaging platform widely used legitimately for app engagement. Its free tier with broad reach makes it occasionally attractive for sending spam push notifications. The platform enforces AUP and monitors for abusive sending patterns, but push notification spam is a documented misuse vector for engagement-bait campaigns.

OneStat.history: OneStat is a web analytics service that was active in the early 2000s. It appears to have declined significantly and may now be largely inactive.
OneStat.summary_abuse: OneStat is a legacy web analytics service with no notable abuse signal.

OneTag.history: Founded in 2015 by Daniel Pirchio and Arnaldo Resega in Italy, OneTag grew as a programmatic advertising SSP/DSP. It was acquired by Wise Equity in September 2022. In 2026, Onetag acquired the Italian ad tech company Aryel to expand into immersive advertising.
OneTag.summary_abuse: OneTag is a programmatic advertising platform. Ad-tech platforms are a common vector for malvertising — where malicious ads are served through legitimate ad networks. As a SSP/DSP platform OneTag's inventory may include third-party publisher sites where ad quality controls vary, carrying inherent malvertising risk common to programmatic ad networks.

OneTrust.history: Founded in 2016 by Kabir Barday in Atlanta focused on GDPR compliance tooling. OneTrust achieved unicorn status with a $1.3B valuation after its Series A in 2019, raised $1.13B total, and grew to a $5.1B valuation after its Series C in 2020. It acquired Convercent in 2021 to expand into ethics and compliance, and received a $150M Series D in 2023.
OneTrust.summary_abuse: OneTrust is a privacy and compliance management platform used by large enterprises. It has no notable abuse signal — its consent management, privacy, and data governance tools are deployed by organizations to reduce regulatory risk, not surfaces fraudsters exploit.

Oney.history: Founded in 1983 in France by Gérard Mulliez of Auchan Group, Oney pioneered split payment solutions in Europe and launched the 3x/4x payment method in 2008. It operates in 10 European countries and is jointly owned by Groupe BPCE (50.1%) and ELO (49.9%).
Oney.summary_abuse: Oney is a regulated European payment and consumer finance provider. As a licensed bank subsidiary operating across Europe, it has no notable abuse signal and is subject to standard financial regulatory oversight.

Onfido.history: Founded in July 2012 by Oxford graduates Husayn Kassai, Eamon Jubbawy, and Ruhul Amin, Onfido pioneered AI-powered identity document verification. After raising over $200M and growing to 500+ employees and $140M+ ARR, it was acquired by Entrust Corporation in April 2024 for $650M in an all-cash deal.
Onfido.summary_abuse: Onfido is an identity verification platform used to prevent fraud, not enable it. It has no notable abuse signal and is a core anti-fraud infrastructure component for financial institutions and fintechs.

ONiAD.history: ONiAD was founded in 2017 and is based in Zaragoza, Spain, providing omnichannel digital advertising technology including display, native, video, audio, and DOOH formats. No notable ownership changes are documented.
ONiAD.summary_abuse: ONiAD is a digital advertising platform that, like other ad networks, could be misused for malvertising campaigns or fraudulent ad traffic. No specific abuse incidents are publicly documented, but its role as an ad broker places it in a moderate-risk category relative to pure SaaS tools.

Onicon.history: Onicon is a Russian live chat software developed by Megagroup, a web services company. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
Onicon.summary_abuse: Onicon is a Russian-developed live chat SaaS with no documented abuse signal.

Onlim.history: Onlim was founded in 2015 by Alexander Wahler, Corneliu Valentin Stanciu, and Ioan Toma in Telfs, Austria, as a spin-off from the University of Innsbruck. The company specialises in knowledge-driven conversational AI, building chatbots and voice assistants for enterprises in the DACH region, with particular expertise in combining knowledge graphs with NLP.
Onlim.summary_abuse: Onlim is an Austrian enterprise conversational AI platform with no documented abuse history. It provides chatbot and voice assistant solutions to businesses; the platform is not accessible for public or anonymous use and has no known phishing or fraud associations.

Online Chat Centers.history: Online Chat Centers is a live chat SaaS for website customer support integration. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
Online Chat Centers.summary_abuse: Online Chat Centers is a live chat SaaS with no documented abuse signal.

Online Succes.history: Online Succes is a Dutch marketing automation and website visitor tracking platform targeting Dutch SME businesses, offering lead identification and marketing automation to help businesses convert website traffic into qualified leads.
Online Succes.summary_abuse: Online Succes is a Dutch B2B marketing automation and visitor tracking tool. It collects behavioural data from website visitors, giving it a moderate privacy surface. There is no documented abuse history, and as a subscription B2B product targeting Dutch businesses, anonymous or fraudulent use is unlikely.

onlyfy Application Manager.history: onlyfy Application Manager was formerly known as Prescreen, an applicant tracking system. It was acquired by XING (now New Work SE), the German-speaking professional network, and rebranded as onlyfy as part of XING's HR product suite.
onlyfy Application Manager.summary_abuse: onlyfy Application Manager is an ATS used by employers for recruiting, with no notable abuse signal. It handles personal data of job applicants in compliance with GDPR, and has no consumer-facing features that would attract fraudsters.

onpublix.history: onpublix is a German web CMS for eBusinesses; no notable ownership changes or deprecation events are documented.
onpublix.summary_abuse: onpublix is a German CMS platform with no notable abuse signal.

Onsen UI.history: Created in 2013 by Asial Corporation (Monaca) in Japan as an open-source mobile UI framework for Cordova and PhoneGap hybrid apps. Version 2.0 was released in 2016 with a Web Components-based architecture decoupled from specific JS frameworks.
Onsen UI.summary_abuse: Onsen UI is an open-source mobile UI component library with no abuse signal. It is a frontend framework used by developers and has no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.

OnShop.history: OnShop is a niche ecommerce platform for online merchants with no public pricing. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
OnShop.summary_abuse: OnShop is an ecommerce SaaS with no documented abuse signal.

OnSip.history: Founded in 2004 as Junction Networks, the company rebranded to OnSIP and has delivered hosted VoIP and cloud PBX services using patented technology for over 20 years. No major acquisitions or ownership changes are publicly documented.
OnSip.summary_abuse: OnSip is a legitimate business VoIP and live-chat platform with no notable abuse signal. VoIP services carry some risk of robocall or toll-fraud abuse in general, but OnSip has no documented history of enabling such activities at scale.

Onsite Support.history: Onsite Support is an AI-enhanced helpdesk and live chat platform. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
Onsite Support.summary_abuse: Onsite Support is an AI helpdesk SaaS with no documented abuse signal.

OnUniverse.history: Founded in 2014 by Joseph Cohen after selling his ed-tech startup Lore, Universe (marketed as OnUniverse) is the first mobile-native website builder built for iPhone and iPad. It was part of Y Combinator's W18 batch and raised $10M Series A from Google Ventures in 2020 and $30M Series B in 2021.
OnUniverse.summary_abuse: OnUniverse is a mobile website builder with a free tier that lets users create publicly accessible websites. This carries moderate risk for fraudulent landing pages or phishing sites, though its mobile-only editing paradigm limits appeal to sophisticated bad actors. No major abuse incidents are publicly documented.

Ookla Speedtest Custom.history: Ookla was founded in 2006 in Seattle by Mike Apgar and Doug Suttles, launching Speedtest.net as the de facto internet speed testing standard. Ziff Davis acquired Ookla in 2014. The Speedtest Custom product replaced Flash-based Speedtest Mini in 2016. In 2026, Accenture announced an acquisition of Ookla from Ziff Davis for $1.2 billion.
Ookla Speedtest Custom.summary_abuse: Ookla Speedtest Custom is a network speed testing widget embedded on ISP and enterprise sites. It has no notable abuse signal as it serves a straightforward diagnostics function with no hosting or content publishing surface.

Oopy.history: Oopy is a South Korean SaaS product that turns Notion pages into websites with custom domains. Founded around 2020, it allows users to create simple public websites from Notion documents without coding.
Oopy.summary_abuse: Oopy allows users to publish public websites from Notion pages using custom domains, which creates some risk for hosting phishing landing pages or fraudulent content. The ease of setup and custom domain support could be exploited, though no specific documented abuse incidents are known.

Opal.history: Opal is an open-source Ruby-to-JavaScript transpiler that allows Ruby code to run in web browsers. It has been community-maintained since around 2012 with no notable corporate backing or ownership changes.
Opal.summary_abuse: Opal is a Ruby-to-JavaScript transpiler with no abuse signal. It is a developer tool with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.

Open AdStream.history: Open AdStream is an ad serving platform that became part of the Xaxis programmatic advertising ecosystem under WPP. Originally a standalone ad server, it was absorbed into Xaxis's programmatic offering.
Open AdStream.summary_abuse: Open AdStream is an ad serving platform. Like all ad networks, it carries inherent malvertising risk where bad actors may attempt to inject malicious ads through the programmatic supply chain. This is a standard risk across ad-tech platforms rather than a specific documented incident.

Open Classifieds.history: Open Classifieds is an open-source PHP classifieds platform that allows operators to run classified ad websites. It has a CPE registration indicating some historical tracking in vulnerability databases. No notable acquisitions or major ownership changes are documented.
Open Classifieds.summary_abuse: Open Classifieds is a self-hosted classified ads platform. As with any open classifieds system, it may be deployed to host illicit secondhand goods listings, but the software itself is a neutral tool and no platform-level abuse is documented.

Open edX.history: Open edX was launched in 2012 as a joint venture by MIT and Harvard University as the open-source backbone of edX, one of the largest online learning platforms. edX was acquired by 2U in 2021, but the Open edX platform continued as a community open-source project under the Open edX nonprofit.
Open edX.summary_abuse: Open edX is an open-source learning management system with no notable abuse signal. It is used by universities and enterprises for online course delivery and has no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters typically target.

Open eShop.history: Open eShop is a small open-source PHP ecommerce platform. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented; it appears to have low activity.
Open eShop.summary_abuse: Open eShop is a niche open-source ecommerce script with no documented abuse signal.

Open Graph.history: The Open Graph protocol was introduced by Facebook in 2010 to enable any web page to become a rich object in a social graph. Now universally used for social media link previews.
Open Graph.summary_abuse: Open Graph is a web metadata standard with no abuse signal.

Open Journal Systems.history: Open Journal Systems was developed by the Public Knowledge Project (PKP) at Simon Fraser University in Canada, with the first release in 2001. It is the world's most widely used open-source scholarly journal publishing platform, used by thousands of journals globally.
Open Journal Systems.summary_abuse: Open Journal Systems is an academic journal publishing platform with no notable abuse signal. It is used by universities and research institutions for peer-reviewed journal management, with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters target.

Open Web Analytics.history: Open Web Analytics is an open-source, self-hosted alternative to Google Analytics, developed and maintained by Peter Adams since around 2006. It has remained a small community-driven project with no notable corporate backing.
Open Web Analytics.summary_abuse: Open Web Analytics is a self-hosted web analytics tool with no notable abuse signal. Its self-hosted nature means any risks are isolated to the deployer's infrastructure.

Open-Props.history: Open-Props is an open-source CSS custom properties library created by Adam Argyle (a Google Chrome team member) around 2021 to accelerate design consistency using CSS variables.
Open-Props.summary_abuse: Open-Props is a CSS utility library with no abuse signal. It is a developer design tool with no consumer-facing surface.

Open-Xchange App Suite.history: Open-Xchange was founded in 2005 in Germany as a web-based groupware and email platform, heavily used by European ISPs and hosting providers as a white-label email and collaboration suite. It expanded into document editing with OX Text and built a sovereign cloud offering.
Open-Xchange App Suite.summary_abuse: Open-Xchange App Suite is a business email and collaboration platform. As a hosted email platform it carries standard spam risk, but it is primarily deployed by ISPs and enterprises with appropriate abuse controls. No notable documented abuse incidents.

OpenBSD httpd.history: OpenBSD httpd is the built-in web server of the OpenBSD operating system, introduced in OpenBSD 5.6 (2014) as a security-focused alternative to external web servers. It is maintained by the OpenBSD project.
OpenBSD httpd.summary_abuse: OpenBSD httpd is a web server included with OpenBSD, known for its security-first design. It has no notable abuse signal and is used primarily by security-conscious administrators.

OpenCart.history: OpenCart was created by Christopher G. Mann and first released in 2005, with a major rewrite released in 2008 under the GPL-3.0 license. It grew to become one of the more widely used open-source PHP ecommerce platforms globally, particularly popular with small to medium businesses.
OpenCart.summary_abuse: OpenCart stores are occasionally targeted for Magecart-style card-skimming attacks due to their payment processing surfaces, but the software itself has no notable abuse profile as a tool used by malicious actors. Administrators running outdated versions have historically faced SQL injection vulnerabilities. Legitimate use overwhelmingly dominates.

OpenChat.history: OpenChat (oc.app) is a privacy-focused community chat application. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
OpenChat.summary_abuse: OpenChat is a privacy-focused chat application. Community-owned chat platforms can be used for coordinating illicit activities, but no specific documented abuse incidents are known for this platform.

OpenCities.history: OpenCities was an Australian government-focused CMS vendor acquired by Granicus, a US-based government technology platform company. Under Granicus, it became part of the GovAccess product suite focused on digital government services.
OpenCities.summary_abuse: OpenCities is a government CMS platform with no notable abuse signal. It targets government organizations with controlled procurement processes and has no public-facing free tier or anonymous sign-up that could be exploited.

OpenCms.history: OpenCms was created by Alkacon Software in Germany, with the first version released in 2000. It is a Java-based open-source CMS that has maintained a stable user base in European enterprise markets, with Alkacon offering commercial support alongside the free community edition.
OpenCms.summary_abuse: OpenCms has no notable abuse signal. It is a self-hosted Java CMS primarily used by enterprises and government bodies in Europe, with no consumer-facing free tier that would attract malicious actors.

OpenCV.history: Initiated by Intel Research in 1999 with Gary Bradski as lead, OpenCV released its first alpha at the 2000 IEEE CVPR conference. Support transitioned from Intel to Willow Garage, then Itseez (later re-acquired by Intel), and in 2012 stewardship moved to the OpenCV.org nonprofit foundation.
OpenCV.summary_abuse: OpenCV is a foundational computer vision library with no abuse signal. It is used by researchers, companies, and government agencies for legitimate vision applications.

OpenElement.history: OpenElement is a free PHP-based WYSIWYG website builder and CMS. It is a small open-source project with limited community activity and appears to have been maintained primarily by a small team in the UK.
OpenElement.summary_abuse: OpenElement has no notable abuse signal. It is a small self-hosted website builder with minimal footprint and no consumer-facing hosted service that could be exploited.

OpenGrok.history: OpenGrok is an open-source source-code search and cross-reference engine originally created at Sun Microsystems around 2005 and released under the CDDL license. After Oracle acquired Sun in 2010, the project was transferred to GitHub and continues to be maintained by a small community. It is used primarily within large engineering organizations for code navigation.
OpenGrok.summary_abuse: OpenGrok is a self-hosted developer code-search tool with no consumer-facing surface and no abuse signal.

OpenGSE.history: OpenGSE was a Google open-source project providing a Java servlet engine and compliance test suite, originally hosted on Google Code. The project appears to have been abandoned with no active development since the early 2010s.
OpenGSE.summary_abuse: OpenGSE is a defunct developer test suite with no consumer surface and no abuse signal.

OpenLayers.history: OpenLayers is an open-source JavaScript mapping library initially released in 2006 by MetaCarta. It has been maintained by the open-source geospatial community since then and is one of the standard libraries for web mapping applications.
OpenLayers.summary_abuse: OpenLayers is a JavaScript mapping library with no abuse signal. It is a developer tool used to build web mapping applications.

OpenNemas.history: OpenNemas is a Spanish CMS, likely targeting news and digital publishing. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
OpenNemas.summary_abuse: OpenNemas is a niche Spanish CMS with no documented abuse signal.

OpenPanel.history: OpenPanel is an open-source analytics library for developers to track application events, launched around 2023 as a privacy-focused alternative to commercial analytics SDKs.
OpenPanel.summary_abuse: OpenPanel is a developer analytics library with no abuse signal. It is a developer tool with no consumer-facing surface.

OpenPay.history: Openpay is an Australian buy-now-pay-later payment solution founded around 2013, primarily serving the Australian and UK retail markets. It went through financial difficulties and in early 2023 ceased operations in the UK market, though it continued in Australia.
OpenPay.summary_abuse: Openpay is a BNPL payment processor with standard financial-services fraud controls. Payment processors of this type see occasional friendly-fraud (false chargebacks) but no notable phishing or scam-infrastructure abuse signal specific to this provider.

OpenResty.history: OpenResty was created by Yichun Zhang (agentzh) and first released in 2009 as an extension of nginx that integrates LuaJIT for scripting high-performance web applications. It became widely adopted for building API gateways and edge computing services. OpenResty Inc. was founded to provide commercial support.
OpenResty.summary_abuse: OpenResty is a self-hosted nginx-based web platform with no inherent abuse surface. As a server infrastructure tool, its abuse profile depends entirely on how deployers configure it; it has no notable documented abuse signal as a preferred tool for malicious actors.

OpenSSL.history: OpenSSL originated as a fork of SSLeay (developed by Eric Young and Tim Hudson) in December 1998. After the 2014 Heartbleed vulnerability exposed critical under-resourcing, the Core Infrastructure Initiative provided funding and governance improved. In 2024, management was divided between the OpenSSL Foundation (open-source) and OpenSSL Corporation (commercial support).
OpenSSL.summary_abuse: OpenSSL is a foundational cryptography and TLS library with no abuse signal in the sense that fraudsters target it — it is infrastructure. Vulnerabilities in OpenSSL (like Heartbleed) have historically had broad impact, but the library itself is not misused by scammers.

OpenStreetMap.history: OpenStreetMap was founded by Steve Coast in the UK in 2004 as a crowdsourced alternative to proprietary map data. The OpenStreetMap Foundation was established in 2006 to support the project. It is now the dominant open geographic database used worldwide, underpinning commercial services by Apple, Facebook, Amazon, and many others.
OpenStreetMap.summary_abuse: OpenStreetMap has minimal abuse signal as a crowd-sourced geographic data project. Occasional vandalism of map data by bad actors has occurred but is reverted by the active community. The data itself has no consumer-facing free-hosting or custom-domain features that scammers typically exploit.

OpenSwoole.history: OpenSwoole was forked from the Swoole project in 2021 by a group of community contributors who sought a more open and community-driven governance model. The original Swoole project is maintained by a Chinese company; OpenSwoole operates as an independent fork under Apache-2.0.
OpenSwoole.summary_abuse: OpenSwoole is a PHP coroutine framework with no consumer-facing surface and no abuse signal.

OpenTable.history: OpenTable was founded in San Francisco in 1998 by Sid Gorham, Eric Moe, and Chuck Templeton as one of the first online restaurant reservation platforms. It went public in 2009 and was acquired by Priceline (now Booking Holdings) in 2014 for $2.6 billion.
OpenTable.summary_abuse: OpenTable handles consumer restaurant reservations and PII including emails and dining preferences. No significant abuse of the platform as infrastructure for fraud has been documented, but as a consumer data platform it has appeared in phishing lures impersonating reservation confirmations. The parent company Booking Holdings has standard enterprise-grade anti-abuse posture.

opentelemetry.history: OpenTelemetry was formed in 2019 through the merger of OpenCensus (from Google) and OpenTracing, two previously competing observability frameworks. It is hosted by the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF) and has rapidly become the industry standard for distributed tracing, metrics, and logs in cloud-native applications.
opentelemetry.summary_abuse: OpenTelemetry has no notable abuse signal; it is a backend observability framework with no consumer-facing surface. It is used by developers to instrument applications for monitoring.

OpenText Web.history: OpenText is a Canadian enterprise software company founded in 1991. The OpenText Web CMS product evolved from various acquisitions including Recommind, HP Autonomy IDOL and others. OpenText is publicly traded on NASDAQ and TSX.
OpenText Web.summary_abuse: OpenText Web CMS is an enterprise content management product used by large organizations. It has no consumer-facing surface and no notable abuse signal.

OpenText Web Solutions.history: OpenText Web Solutions is a web content management product from OpenText Corporation, a Canadian enterprise software company founded in 1991 and publicly traded on NASDAQ and TSX. The Web Solutions product line focuses on ASP.NET-based CMS for corporate websites.
OpenText Web Solutions.summary_abuse: OpenText Web Solutions is an enterprise CMS product with no consumer-facing surface and no notable abuse signal.

OpenTiendas.history: OpenTiendas is a Spanish no-code hosted ecommerce platform. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
OpenTiendas.summary_abuse: OpenTiendas is a hosted ecommerce SaaS. As with all hosted store builders, it could potentially be used to create fraudulent shops, but no specific documented incidents are known.

Opentracker.history: Opentracker is a small Dutch web analytics SaaS founded around 2001, offering real-time visitor tracking and behavioral analytics for websites. No notable ownership changes or deprecation events are documented.
Opentracker.summary_abuse: Opentracker is a web analytics platform with no notable abuse signal. It is a niche B2B SaaS focused on website visitor tracking, without consumer-facing content hosting or anonymous signup features that would attract fraudsters.

OpenUI5.history: OpenUI5 was released by SAP SE in 2013 as the open-source counterpart to the proprietary SAPUI5 framework under the Apache 2.0 license. It provides a comprehensive JavaScript UI component library for building enterprise web applications, and is widely used within the SAP ecosystem.
OpenUI5.summary_abuse: OpenUI5 has no notable abuse signal. It is a backend UI framework for enterprise application development with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.

OpenWeb.history: OpenWeb was founded in 2012 (originally as Spot.IM) in Israel, offering a comment and community engagement platform for publishers. The company rebranded to OpenWeb around 2021 and shifted focus to social engagement and advertising technology for media companies.
OpenWeb.summary_abuse: OpenWeb is an ad-tech and community engagement platform. As with most advertising technology, it collects user behavioral data for ad targeting purposes. Its use in comment sections on news sites creates some potential for comment spam and coordinated inauthentic behavior, but there is no notable documented abuse of the platform for phishing or fraud.

OpenX.history: OpenX was founded in 2008 as a programmatic advertising exchange and ad server. In 2020, OpenX agreed to pay $2 million to settle FTC charges related to illegally collecting children's data and selling location data. The company has remained independent and continues to operate as a major programmatic advertising platform.
OpenX.summary_abuse: OpenX is a programmatic advertising platform that, as part of the ad-tech ecosystem, collects detailed behavioral and location data on users. In 2020, the FTC penalized OpenX $2 million for collecting data on children and selling precise location data. Ad networks like OpenX are occasionally used for malvertising campaigns, where malicious ads are served through legitimate ad exchange infrastructure. The platform has no consumer-facing signup surface for fraudsters to exploit directly.

OperateBeyond.history: OperateBeyond is a small software company offering CRM, inventory management, and dealer website solutions, targeting automotive dealers. No notable ownership changes or milestones are publicly documented.
OperateBeyond.summary_abuse: OperateBeyond is a niche CRM and dealer-website platform with no notable abuse signal.

Opesta.history: Opesta is a small marketing automation platform focused on Facebook Messenger chatbots and lead nurturing. It serves small businesses running Facebook ad campaigns.
Opesta.summary_abuse: Opesta is a Facebook Messenger marketing automation tool. Messaging automation platforms of this type can be misused for unsolicited messaging campaigns, though Opesta targets legitimate small business marketers and requires a Facebook account for access.

Opigno LMS.history: Opigno LMS was first released in 2013 by Connect-i, a Belgian IT consultancy. It is built on the Drupal CMS framework and provides an open-source learning management system for creating and delivering online courses. No notable ownership changes or deprecation events are documented.
Opigno LMS.summary_abuse: Opigno LMS has no notable abuse signal. It is an open-source learning management system intended for self-hosted deployments, with no consumer-facing hosted service that fraudsters would target.

OpinionBar.history: OpinionBar is a small SaaS platform offering embeddable survey and feedback forms with a cash payment incentive model for respondents.
OpinionBar.summary_abuse: OpinionBar is a survey/feedback platform with cash-payment incentives for participants. Incentivized survey platforms can attract fraudulent respondents attempting to collect payments without providing genuine feedback, but the platform's abuse risk to third parties is low.

OpinionLab.history: OpinionLab was founded in 1999 as an omnichannel customer feedback platform, becoming well established in enterprise voice-of-customer solutions. The company was acquired by Verint Systems, a customer engagement intelligence company, around 2016.
OpinionLab.summary_abuse: OpinionLab has no notable abuse signal. It is an enterprise B2B customer feedback platform with no consumer-facing content hosting or anonymous access that fraudsters would target.

OpnForm.history: OpnForm is an open-source-inspired form builder launched around 2022, offering both a hosted SaaS tier and a self-hostable version. It is a small team product positioned as an alternative to Typeform.
OpnForm.summary_abuse: OpnForm is a form builder with a free tier that allows form creation and hosting. Form builders with free tiers can be used to create phishing credential-harvesting forms, though OpnForm is a small platform without the scale that typically attracts systematic abuse. No specific documented abuse incidents are known.

Ops Calendar.history: Ops Calendar is a small SaaS marketing calendar tool for managing content and social media publishing workflows.
Ops Calendar.summary_abuse: Ops Calendar is a marketing planning tool with no consumer-facing surface and no notable abuse signal.

Optibase.history: Optibase is a small A/B testing SaaS product built specifically for Webflow sites, launched around 2022.
Optibase.summary_abuse: Optibase is a niche A/B testing tool for Webflow with no notable abuse signal.

Optimise.history: Optimise Media Group was founded in 2004 in the UK as a performance advertising and affiliate network. The company has remained independent and operates primarily across Asia-Pacific and Europe. No notable ownership changes are documented.
Optimise.summary_abuse: Optimise Media is an affiliate and performance advertising network. Affiliate networks can be exploited for commission fraud, cookie stuffing, and promoting low-quality or scam offers, which are common abuse vectors in the performance marketing industry. However, there are no documented notable incidents specific to Optimise Media.

Optimization Detective.history: Optimization Detective is a WordPress plugin from the official WordPress Performance Team, first released in 2023. It captures real user metrics about page elements across device types to apply loading optimizations such as lazy loading and resource prioritization.
Optimization Detective.summary_abuse: Optimization Detective has no abuse signal. It is a performance optimization plugin maintained by the WordPress core team, operating entirely server-side with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.

Optimizely.history: Optimizely was founded in 2010 by Dan Siroker and Pete Koomen, both former Google employees, as a pioneering A/B testing and experimentation platform. In 2017, it acquired Episerver's CMS business, and in 2019, Episerver (now owned by Insight Partners) acquired Optimizely. The combined company rebranded to Optimizely in 2021, absorbing the Episerver product line into a unified digital experience platform.
Optimizely.summary_abuse: Optimizely has no notable abuse signal. It is an enterprise A/B testing and experimentation platform used by large organizations to run controlled experiments on digital properties. It has no consumer-facing content hosting surface that fraudsters would target.

Optimizely Commerce.history: Optimizely Commerce is the B2C e-commerce product within the Optimizely digital experience platform. It originated from the Episerver Commerce module, which was folded into the Optimizely brand in 2021 after Episerver (owned by Insight Partners) acquired Optimizely and rebranded the combined company.
Optimizely Commerce.summary_abuse: Optimizely Commerce has no notable abuse signal. It is an enterprise e-commerce and content management platform deployed by established businesses; the platform does not expose consumer-facing hosting that scammers would leverage for phishing or fraud.

Optimizely Content Management.history: Optimizely Content Management originated as EPiServer, founded in Sweden in 1994 as one of the earliest enterprise CMS platforms. EPiServer later acquired Ektron in 2015 and merged with the Optimizely experimentation platform in 2021 under the Optimizely brand following Insight Partners' acquisition. The product is now marketed as Optimizely CMS.
Optimizely Content Management.summary_abuse: Optimizely Content Management has no notable abuse signal. It is an enterprise CMS deployed by established organizations and does not expose consumer-facing hosting that fraudsters would exploit.

Optimole.history: Optimole was launched around 2018 by Themeisle, a Romanian WordPress theme and plugin company. It provides cloud-based image optimization and delivery for WordPress sites. No notable ownership changes beyond the parent company relationship are documented.
Optimole.summary_abuse: Optimole has no notable abuse signal. It is an image optimization CDN for WordPress sites with no consumer-facing content hosting that fraudsters would target.

OptiMonk.history: OptiMonk was founded in 2014 in Hungary as a conversion optimization and exit-intent popup tool. It has grown into a mainstream on-site messaging platform used by e-commerce businesses worldwide. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes are documented.
OptiMonk.summary_abuse: OptiMonk is a popup and on-site messaging platform for e-commerce. Popup and cart-abandonment tools can create aggressive or dark-pattern-adjacent user experiences such as difficult-to-close overlays and manipulative urgency messaging, though these are design choices by the deploying business rather than OptiMonk itself being abusive. No documented incidents of the platform being used for phishing or fraud.

Optimove.history: Optimove was founded in 2009 in Israel as a customer data platform and relationship marketing tool, using predictive micro-segmentation and AI-driven campaign automation. The company has raised substantial VC funding and grown into a mainstream CDP. No major acquisitions or rebrands are documented.
Optimove.summary_abuse: Optimove has no notable abuse signal. It is an enterprise customer data platform and marketing automation tool used by brands to personalize communications to their own customers. It has no anonymous signup surface or consumer-facing content hosting that fraudsters would exploit.

Optimx Sports.history: OptimX Sports is a small SaaS platform providing website building tools targeted at amateur and semi-professional sports teams, particularly in hockey, rugby, and lacrosse.
Optimx Sports.summary_abuse: OptimX Sports is a niche sports team website builder with no notable abuse signal.

Optimy.history: Optimy is a Belgian CSR and social impact management platform founded around 2012, offering tools for managing grants, sponsorships, volunteering, and CSR programs for corporate clients.
Optimy.summary_abuse: Optimy is a B2B CSR management platform used by corporations for social program management. It has no consumer-facing surface and no notable abuse signal.

OptinMonster.history: OptinMonster was founded in 2013 by Syed Balkhi and Thomas Griffin as a lead generation and email list growth platform. It became part of the Awesome Motive family of WordPress products. The company pioneered exit-intent popup technology for e-commerce and has grown to serve millions of websites.
OptinMonster.summary_abuse: OptinMonster is a lead generation popup platform. Popup tools can contribute to manipulative UX patterns like aggressive email capture overlays and difficult-to-close modals, though these represent business choices by site owners rather than platform-level abuse. The platform collects visitor behavioral data for segmentation. No notable incidents of OptinMonster being used directly for phishing or fraud.

OptinMonster plugin.history: The OptinMonster WordPress plugin is the free WordPress.org companion plugin for the OptinMonster lead generation SaaS, launched around 2013 by Awesome Motive. It connects WordPress sites to the OptinMonster service for popup and lead capture management.
OptinMonster plugin.summary_abuse: The OptinMonster WordPress plugin connects sites to the OptinMonster lead generation service and has no notable abuse signal on its own. Popup lead generation tools can be misused to create aggressive or misleading opt-in experiences, but the plugin itself is a widely-used legitimate tool with active WordPress.org support.

Optinopoli.history: Optinopoli is a small lead-capture and marketing conversion SaaS product with no notable public history or ownership changes.
Optinopoli.summary_abuse: Optinopoli is a lead-capture platform. Such tools can be deployed to collect email addresses at scale for spam purposes, though Optinopoli is a small niche vendor without a documented abuse record.

OpTuNE.history: OpTuNE is an event booking and management platform for artists and entertainers. No notable ownership or acquisition events are documented.
OpTuNE.summary_abuse: OpTuNE is a niche event booking SaaS for artists with no documented abuse signal.

Oracle Application Express.history: Oracle APEX (Application Express) was originally called HTML DB and first released in 2004 as an enterprise low-code development platform built into the Oracle Database. Oracle renamed it to Application Express (APEX) and has maintained it as a no-cost feature of Oracle Database since then, making it widely adopted in Oracle database shops for rapid application development.
Oracle Application Express.summary_abuse: Oracle APEX has no notable abuse signal. It is an enterprise low-code development platform integrated with Oracle Database, used by development teams within organizations. It has no anonymous consumer-facing content hosting that fraudsters would exploit.

Oracle Application Server.history: Oracle Application Server (OAS, also known as Oracle iAS) was Oracle's J2EE application server product, first released around 2000 as Oracle's response to IBM WebSphere and Sun's Java EE stack. Oracle has since deprecated OAS in favor of Oracle WebLogic Server following the acquisition of BEA Systems in 2008.
Oracle Application Server.summary_abuse: Oracle Application Server has no notable abuse signal. It is a deprecated enterprise application server product deployed within organizational data centers, with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.

Oracle BlueKai.history: BlueKai was founded in 2007 as an independent data management platform (DMP) and advertising data marketplace. Oracle acquired BlueKai in 2014 for approximately $400 million, rebranding it as Oracle Data Cloud and later Oracle BlueKai. In 2020, a security incident exposed a database containing billions of records of personal consumer data collected through its tracking infrastructure.
Oracle BlueKai.summary_abuse: Oracle BlueKai is a data management platform that tracks user behavior across the web for advertising targeting. In 2020, a major security incident exposed billions of personal data records from its tracking database due to an unsecured server. As a data broker and ad-tech platform, it collects detailed behavioral profiles on consumers without direct opt-in, raising significant privacy concerns. It is not typically used by external threat actors for fraud but is itself a subject of privacy criticism.

Oracle Commerce.history: Oracle Commerce originated as ATG (Art Technology Group) Web Commerce, founded in 1991 and publicly traded before Oracle acquired ATG in 2011. Oracle integrated the platform into its Customer Experience suite, merging it with Endeca (also acquired in 2011) to create Oracle Commerce, which supports unified B2B and B2C e-commerce scenarios.
Oracle Commerce.summary_abuse: Oracle Commerce has no notable abuse signal. It is an enterprise e-commerce platform deployed by established businesses on their own infrastructure or Oracle cloud, with no anonymous consumer-facing content hosting that fraudsters would exploit.

Oracle Commerce Cloud.history: Oracle Commerce Cloud is the cloud-native SaaS version of Oracle's e-commerce offering, launched around 2015 as part of Oracle Cloud. It is built on the foundation of the Oracle Commerce platform (originally ATG) and delivered as a fully managed SaaS service supporting B2C and B2B scenarios on Oracle Cloud Infrastructure.
Oracle Commerce Cloud.summary_abuse: Oracle Commerce Cloud has no notable abuse signal. It is an enterprise e-commerce SaaS platform deployed by established retailers and B2B organizations, without anonymous consumer-facing content hosting that fraudsters would exploit.

Oracle Content Management.history: Oracle Content Management is a cloud-based CMS and digital asset management platform launched by Oracle around 2018 as part of Oracle Cloud. It targets enterprise organizations needing structured content management and integration with other Oracle Cloud applications.
Oracle Content Management.summary_abuse: Oracle Content Management has no notable abuse signal. It is an enterprise CMS deployed by organizations within Oracle Cloud, with no consumer-facing content hosting that fraudsters would exploit.

Oracle Dynamic Monitoring Service.history: Oracle Dynamic Monitoring Service (DMS) is a built-in feature of Oracle WebLogic Server that provides real-time performance metrics and diagnostics for Java EE applications. It has been part of the WebLogic platform since at least the early 2010s.
Oracle Dynamic Monitoring Service.summary_abuse: Oracle Dynamic Monitoring Service has no abuse signal. It is an internal monitoring component of Oracle WebLogic Server used for application performance diagnostics within enterprise environments, with no consumer-facing surface.

Oracle HTTP Server.history: Oracle HTTP Server is Oracle's enterprise web server, built on the Apache HTTP Server codebase, introduced as part of Oracle Application Server in the late 1990s and later integrated into Oracle Fusion Middleware. It serves as the front-end listener for Oracle WebLogic Server deployments and has tracked Apache HTTP Server releases through the 11g and 12c product generations. No ownership changes — Oracle Corporation has always owned it.
Oracle HTTP Server.summary_abuse: Oracle HTTP Server has no notable abuse signal. It is a backend enterprise web server deployed in on-premises Oracle Fusion Middleware environments with no consumer-facing hosting surface that would attract phishing or scam operators.

Oracle Infinity.history: Oracle acquired the Infinity big data analytics platform and integrated it into its Marketing Cloud as Oracle Infinity Behavioral Intelligence, rebranding and expanding it for enterprise digital analytics. It remains part of Oracle CX Marketing and is actively maintained as a SaaS offering.
Oracle Infinity.summary_abuse: Oracle Infinity is an enterprise analytics platform with no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B SaaS tool sold to large enterprises for tracking website visitor behavior, with no consumer-facing surface that would be targeted by phishing or fraud operators.

Oracle Maxymiser.history: Maxymiser was founded in 2006 as an A/B testing and multivariate optimization company originally developed in Ukraine. Oracle acquired it in September 2015 to fill a gap in its Marketing Cloud against Adobe Target and Salesforce personalization tools. Oracle has since been sunsetting Maxymiser in favor of other personalization approaches within its CX suite.
Oracle Maxymiser.summary_abuse: Oracle Maxymiser has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B personalization and A/B testing platform used by enterprise marketing teams, with no consumer-facing surface that would attract phishing or fraud operators.

Oracle Moat Measurement.history: Moat was founded in 2010 in New York as an ad measurement and attention analytics company. Oracle acquired Moat for approximately $850 million in April 2017 and integrated it into Oracle Data Cloud as Oracle Moat Measurement, giving Oracle its first dedicated digital advertising measurement capability.
Oracle Moat Measurement.summary_abuse: Oracle Moat Measurement has no notable abuse signal. It is an enterprise B2B advertising measurement and analytics platform used by brands, agencies, and publishers to verify ad viewability and effectiveness, with no consumer-facing surface targeted by fraud operators.

Oracle Recommendations On Demand.history: Oracle Recommendations On Demand is a cloud-based product recommendations engine that was part of the Oracle ATG Commerce platform, providing collaborative filtering and personalized suggestions for ecommerce storefronts. It has been superseded by native recommendation features within Oracle Commerce Cloud and is no longer actively marketed as a standalone product.
Oracle Recommendations On Demand.summary_abuse: Oracle Recommendations On Demand has no notable abuse signal. It is a backend ecommerce recommendation engine embedded in Oracle ATG Commerce deployments, with no consumer-facing surface that fraud operators would target.

Oracle Web Cache.history: Oracle Web Cache was a content-caching server included in Oracle Application Server and early Oracle Fusion Middleware releases, designed to accelerate web site performance. Oracle deprecated and removed it from Fusion Middleware 12c, with 11.1.1.9 being the final supported version.
Oracle Web Cache.summary_abuse: Oracle Web Cache has no notable abuse signal. It is a deprecated backend caching server that was deployed in Oracle enterprise middleware environments with no consumer-facing surface relevant to phishing or fraud.

Oracle WebLogic Server.history: Oracle WebLogic Server originated from BEA Systems, which launched WebLogic as one of the first Java application servers in the mid-1990s. BEA WebLogic became the dominant Java EE application server for enterprises before Oracle acquired BEA Systems for $8.5 billion in 2008, rebranding it as Oracle WebLogic Server and integrating it as the core runtime of Oracle Fusion Middleware.
Oracle WebLogic Server.summary_abuse: Oracle WebLogic Server has no notable consumer-facing abuse signal; however, it is a historically high-value target for exploitation. Multiple critical deserialization and remote code execution vulnerabilities (CVE-2017-10271, CVE-2019-2725, and others) have been actively exploited by ransomware groups and cryptocurrency mining malware to compromise enterprise servers. Oracle patches these actively and enterprises are responsible for timely patching.

Orama.history: Orama was initially released as Lyra, an open-source full-text search engine written in TypeScript, around 2022. The project was renamed Orama and expanded to include vector search, hybrid search, and a cloud hosting platform (Orama Cloud) delivered across a global edge network. The open-source core remains Apache-2.0 licensed with an active community.
Orama.summary_abuse: Orama has no notable abuse signal. It is an open-source search engine library and associated cloud platform used by developers to add search capabilities to applications, with no consumer-facing hosting surface that would attract phishing or fraud operators.

Orankl.history: Orankl is a small San Francisco-based SaaS company offering email marketing automation and customer review collection for ecommerce merchants. It has bootstrapped to over 1,000 customers as of 2021 with no disclosed external funding.
Orankl.summary_abuse: Orankl has no notable abuse signal. It is a small ecommerce email marketing and reviews platform used by small online retailers, with no consumer-facing hosting surface that fraud operators would specifically target.

Orbit Slider.history: Orbit is a jQuery image slider built by ZURB as a playground project, released around 2010. It was widely used during the era of jQuery-based carousels but has since been deprecated as modern CSS and JavaScript alternatives replaced it.
Orbit Slider.summary_abuse: Orbit Slider has no notable abuse signal. It is a deprecated jQuery slider library with no consumer-facing surface that fraud operators would target.

OrbitFox.history: OrbitFox is a free WordPress companion plugin developed by Themeisle, a Romanian WordPress theme and plugin company. It provides integrations and widgets for Elementor, Beaver Builder, and Gutenberg, and is actively maintained alongside Themeisle's premium theme lineup.
OrbitFox.summary_abuse: OrbitFox has no notable abuse signal. It is a free WordPress plugin for page-builder integrations distributed through WordPress.org with standard plugin repository security review.

Orchard Core.history: Orchard Core is a complete rewrite of Orchard CMS (first released 2011 under the .NET Foundation) on ASP.NET Core, started around 2014.
